description.task: Apply the two-pointer technique to solve classic problems efficiently. (1) Find a pair in a sorted array that sums to a target. (2) Remove duplicates from a sorted array in-place. Both run in O(n) time.
instructions: pairSum: while(l<r){int s=arr[l]+arr[r];if(s==target)return new int[]{arr[l],arr[r]};else if(s<target)l++;else r--;}
pairSum on [1,3,5,7,9,11] for target 12: tries 1+11=12 → found immediately → {1,11}
Two-pointer avoids nested loops, reducing time complexity from O(n²) to O(n)
description.hint: pairSum: if(s==target)return new int[]{arr[l],arr[r]};else if(s<target)l++;else r--; removeDups: if(arr[fast]!=arr[slow]){slow++;arr[slow]=arr[fast];}
description.expectedOutput:
Pair summing to 12: [1, 11]
After remove duplicates, len=4
Unique elements: [0, 1, 2, 3]

description.task: Apply the sliding window technique to two classic problems: (1) Maximum sum subarray of fixed size k. (2) Length of longest substring without repeating characters (variable window). Both run in O(n).
description.expectedOutput:
Max sum (k=3): 9
Longest unique 'abcabcbb': 3
Longest unique 'pwwkew':   3

description.task: Demonstrate recognizing algorithm patterns. Implement: (1) Interval merging (sort + sweep) — merge overlapping intervals. (2) Prefix sum for O(1) range queries. Both are core patterns that appear repeatedly across problems.
description.hint: merge: last[1]=Math.max(last[1],intervals[i][1]); prefix: prefix[i+1]=prefix[i]+arr[i]; rangeSum: return prefix[r+1]-prefix[l];
